Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First, I need to find which case_ids have more than six events. To do that, I can group by case_id and count the number of events per case. Then, exclude those case_ids where the count is >6.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So, maybe a subquery or a CTE. In SQL, you can use a subquery in the WHERE clause. So the query would be: SELECT * FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN ( SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6 );

Name: Validation - IND
Text: But wait, in DuckDB, is there any issue with this? I think this should work. Let me check.

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Alternatively, using a CTE: WITH case_counts AS ( SELECT case_id, COUNT(*) as event_count FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <=6 ) SELECT e.* FROM event_log e JOIN case_counts c ON e.case_id = c.case_id. Another way is to use EXISTS. But that might be less efficient. The IN approach is probably better.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Wait, but the HAVING clause in the subquery: when you group by case_id, each group is a case_id, so COUNT(*) gives the number of events per case. So HAVING COUNT(*) <=6 will select those case_ids with <=6 events. Yes, thats correct.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me test with an example. Suppose there are two cases: case1 has 5 events, case2 has 7. The subquery would return case1. Then the main query selects all events where case_id is case1, which are the 5 events. Correct. Another example: case3 has exactly 6 events. It should be included. The HAVING COUNT(*) <=6 includes 6, so yes.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: In DuckDB, the syntax for this should work. DuckDB uses standard SQL, so IN with a subquery is allowed.
